text: Transvaginal rectocele repair reinforced with biological mesh. This technique is indicated in patients with isolated symptomatic anterior rectocele who do not respond to conservative treatment. Complementary tests to support physical examination findings will confirm the real cause of symptoms and discard other associated disorders such as enterocel, colpocele or rectal interception. Physical exam should determine the level, length and width of the rectocele and discard the presence of associated pelvic organ prolapse. Patients with obstructive defecation syndrome and rectocele are further investigated with dynamic MR defecography to assess pelvic floor motion and other structural abnormalities. Patients are unable to expel the contrast retained in the rectocele during evacuation, as can be observed in these two examples.

The surgical field is prepared with the patient in lithotomy position, using a lone star retractor for exposure. Saline and adrenaline solution is injected along the posterior vaginal wall and rectocele to minimize bleeding and facilitate dissection. A transverse vaginal mucosal incision is made above the introitus, and the rectovaginal space is dissected to separate the rectocele from the posterior vaginal wall. Blunt dissection is performed through an avascular space up to the pouch of Douglas and laterally to the pubertalis muscle. Midline longitudinal plication of the rectal wall is performed using absorbable sutures, focusing on the muscular layer without entering the rectal lumen, extending distally to the anal-rectal junction.

The surgeon reinforces the rectovaginal space with a cross-linked acellular porcine dermal collagen mesh, securing it with absorbable sutures placed deep and laterally. Excess graft is trimmed to ensure full coverage. The vaginal wall is then closed with absorbable intermittent sutures, and an intravaginal gauze pack is inserted.

In our experience, if an adequate selection of patients is made, this procedure corrects the symptomatology in eighty percent of patients. We have not observed any complications related to the mesh and the recurrence rate is six percent.
